content.text:
# The Persistence

The cats did not age. This was understood in the building the way certain things are understood—obliquely, without documentation, a fact that lived in the space between knowing and saying.

There were eleven of them on the fourteenth floor, which the residents knew was actually the thirteenth, the numbers skipping the way they do, the superstition built into the architecture itself. The cats moved between apartments through a logic no one had authorized. Doors opened. Doors closed. The cats were on the other side of the doors.

Marisol fed the gray one she called Pachelbel, though she suspected the others fed him too, suspected he answered to a dozen names, a creature of aliases, of distributed identity. He had been gray when she moved in. Her mother had been alive then. Her mother was a name on a stone now, in a cemetery in Queens she visited less than she meant to, and the cat was still gray, still gray in exactly the way he had been, the gray of television snow, of the moment before weather.

"They were here before the renovation," said the man in 14F, whose name she never learned, who she thought of only as the man in 14F, a designation that seemed sufficient, that seemed to contain him. "The 1987 renovation. There are photographs."

"The same cats."

"The board has photographs."

The board met on the second Tuesday. They discussed the boiler, the special assessment, the matter of the lobby mirrors, which were original, which were not to be touched. They did not discuss the cats. The cats were a line item that had been removed from the agenda so many years ago that removing it had itself been forgotten, an absence that had calcified into a kind of presence.

At night Marisol heard them in the walls, or did not hear them, heard instead the building's own sounds and assigned the sounds to the cats, the way you assign meaning to anything that persists. The pipes spoke. The elevator rose and did not stop at her floor. Somewhere a cat was not dying, was performing the ongoing labor of not dying, and she found this comforting and then found her comfort obscene and then stopped examining the feeling because the feeling, like the cats, would simply continue regardless.

She bought the expensive food. The food in the small cans with the foreign words, the food that suggested the sea, that suggested a wholeness available for purchase. Pachelbel ate it or did not eat it. His hunger was theoretical. He watched her with eyes the color of an old transaction and she understood that she was feeding something that did not require feeding, that the ritual was hers, that she was the one being kept.

"Do you ever think," she said to the man in 14F, in the laundry room, the machines turning, the fluorescent hum, "that we're the ones passing through."

He was folding a shirt with great precision.

"The cats own the apartments," he said. "We rent time in them."

The dryer stopped. The silence had a texture. In the silence she could hear, faintly, from the floor above or the floor below or from no floor at all, the sound of something landing softly on four feet, having jumped from a height, having survived the jump, having always survived, intending to survive forever, in this building, on this island, under the indifferent and continuous sky.

---

That's the opening—I leaned into DeLillo's signatures: the deadpan menace, the meditation on death and persistence, the characters who speak in koans, the architecture-as-meaning, the brand names and rituals of consumption. 

Want me to keep going? I could develop the board meeting as a set piece, introduce a resident who becomes obsessed with documenting the cats' immortality, or push further into the paranoid-philosophical register. Let me know what direction appeals to you.
